Nocturnal penile tumescence and rigidity test\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>The nocturnal penile tumescence and rigidity (NPTR) test applies nocturnal monitoring devices that measure the number of erectile episodes, tumescence (circumference change by strain gauges), maximal penile rigidity, and duration of nocturnal erections. The NPTR assessment should be performed on at least two separate nights. A functional erectile mechanism is indicated by an erectile event of at least 60% rigidity recorded on the tip of the penis that lasts for ≥ ten minutes [346]. Nocturnal penile tumescence and rigidity monitoring is an approach for objectively differentiating between organic and psychogenic ED (patients with psychogenic ED usually have normal findings in the NPTR test). However, there are still limitations to NPTR, namely, the true correlation between NPTR and a sex-related erection, the definition used to establish ED and the many potential confounding factors (e.g. situational, age, depression, sleep-related content). This may limit its routine use for diagnostic purposes [346,347].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.5.2. Intracavernous injection test\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>The intracavernous injection test gives limited information about vascular status. A positive test is a rigid erectile response (unable to bend the penis) that appears within ten minutes after the intracavernous injection and lasts for 30 minutes [348]. Overall, the test per se is inconclusive as a diagnostic procedure and a duplex Doppler study of the penis should be requested, if clinically warranted.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.5.3. Dynamic duplex ultrasound of the penis\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Dynamic duplex US of the penis is a second-level diagnostic test that specifically studies the haemodynamic pathophysiology of EF. In clinical practice, it is usually applied when a potential vasculogenic aetiology of ED (e.g. diabetes mellitus, multiple concomitant CV risk factors and\u002For overt peripheral vascular disease, renal transplantation and poor responders to oral therapy) is suspected. Peak systolic blood flow &gt; 30cm\u002Fs, end-diastolic velocity &lt; 3cm\u002Fs and resistance index &gt; 0.8 are usually considered normal [349,350]. Data suggest that duplex scanning as a haemodynamic study may be better at tailoring therapy for ED, such as for low-intensity shock wave treatment (LI-SWT) in men with vasculogenic ED [351]. Further vascular investigation is unnecessary if a duplex US examination is normal. However, it has limitations with regards to the standardised metrics for the haemodynamic parameters utilised, its diagnostic accuracy is challenged by false positive diagnosis due to confounding factors (e.g. anxiety, low vasoactive agent dosage) and its added value to the intracavernous injection test is still unclear [352-355]. Measures such as audiovisual sexual stimulation, high-dosage alprostadil or combination of vasoactive and\u002For oral agents can help improve its accuracy [356-358].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.5.4. Arteriography and dynamic infusion cavernosometry or cavernosography\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Pudendal arteriography should be performed only in patients who are being considered for penile revascularisation [359]. Currently, dynamic infusion cavernosometry or cavernosography are infrequently used diagnostic modalities for the assessment of venogenic ED and the concept of venogenic ED has been questioned. Computed tomography cavernosography is an emerging diagnostic modality providing detailed anatomical information that may aid in diagnosing and planning treatment for ED [360].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.5.5. Psychopathological and psychosocial assessment\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Mental health issues and psychological distress are frequently comorbid with ED [361]. This is most evident for depression and anxiety related disorders, but may also include transitory states of altered mood (i.e. dysfunctional affective states resulting from a specific life stressor or crisis) [278,362,363]. Relationship factors, including lack of satisfaction with the partner, poor sexual relationships, length of the relationship, or feeling emotionally disconnected from the partner during sex, have been related to erectile difficulties and ED [362,364,365]. In contrast, intimacy was found to be a protective factor in ED [267,366]. Additionally, the cognitive factors underpinning organic and non-organic ED (i.e. all dysfunctional thinking styles and expectations about sexuality, poor self-esteem and cognitive distraction from erotic cues) must also be assessed.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Psychosexual assessment in ED cases include a clinical interview considering all the previous topics [367]. Also, self-reported measures are frequently used within the psychosocial context [368]. A growing amount of data suggest that men who have sex with men (MSM) present specific psychological risks associated with erectile capability regarding anal sex [369]. Professionals must tailor their assessment in the context of sexual minorities.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>\u003Cstrong>Figure 5.4: Psychopathological and psychosocial assessment\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003Cimg src=\"https:\u002F\u002Fd56bochluxqnz.cloudfront.net\u002Fguideline-images\u002FSexual-Health-Guidelines-2026-Figure-5-4-Psychopathological-and-psychosocial-assessment.jpg\" alt=\"\">\u003Cstrong>Table 5.2: Indications for specific diagnostic tests for ED and the specific diagnostic tests\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cfigure class=\"table\">\u003Ctable>\u003Ctbody>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Indications for specific diagnostic tests for erectile dysfunction (ED)\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Primary ED (not caused by acquired organic disease or psychogenic disorder).\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Young patients with a history of pelvic or perineal trauma, who could benefit from potentially curative revascularisation surgery or angioplasty.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Patients with penile deformities that might require surgical correction (e.g. Peyronie’s disease and congenital penile curvature).\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Patients with complex psychiatric or psychosexual disorders.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Patients with complex endocrine disorders.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Specific tests may be indicated at the request of the patient or their partner.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Medico-legal reasons (e.g. implantation of penile prosthesis to document end-stage ED, and sexual abuse).\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Specific diagnostic tests for ED\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Nocturnal Penile Tumescence and Rigidity (NTPR) using Rigiscan\u003Csup>®\u003C\u002Fsup>.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cp>Vascular studies,\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Intracavernous vasoactive drug injection,\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Penile dynamic duplex ultrasonography,\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Penile dynamic infusion cavernosometry and cavernosography,\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Internal pudendal arteriography.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Specialised endocrinological studies.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Specialised psycho-diagnostic evaluation.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003C\u002Ftbody>\u003C\u002Ftable>\u003C\u002Ffigure>\u003Ch4>5.5.6. Summary of evidence and recommendations for diagnostic evaluation of erectile dysfunction\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cfigure class=\"table\">\u003Ctable>\u003Ctbody>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Summary of evidence\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>LE\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Medical and sexual history, physical examination and laboratory testing, including metabolic and hormonal profile, may identify risk factors for ED and may help in defining the ED aetiology.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>3\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Validated psychometric questionnaires (e.g. IIEF, EHS) are reliable tools to assess ED severity.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>3\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Specific diagnostic tests could help discern between vasculogenic, hormonal or psychogenic causes\u003Cbr>of ED.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>3\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003C\u002Ftbody>\u003C\u002Ftable>\u003C\u002Ffigure>\u003Cfigure class=\"table\">\u003Ctable>\u003Ctbody>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Recommendations\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Strength rating\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Take a comprehensive medical and sexual history in every patient presenting with erectile dysfunction (ED). Consider psychosexual development, including life stressors, cultural aspects, and cognitive factors regarding patient sexual performance.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Use a validated questionnaire related to ED to assess all sexual function domains (e.g. International Index of Erectile Function) and the effect of a specific treatment modality.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Include a focused physical examination in the initial assessment of men with ED to identify underlying medical conditions and comorbid genital disorders that may be associated with ED.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Evaluate laboratory tests, including glucose and lipid profile and total testosterone, to identify and treat any reversible risk factors and lifestyle factors that can be modified.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Include specific diagnostic tests in the initial evaluation of ED in the presence of the conditions presented in Table 5.2.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003C\u002Ftbody>\u003C\u002Ftable>\u003C\u002Ffigure>\u003Ch3>5.6. Treatment of erectile dysfunction\u003C\u002Fh3>\u003Cp>The Guidelines Panel have developed a comprehensive therapeutic and decision-making algorithm (Figure 5.5) for treating ED. The treatment algorithm was developed as an alternative to the traditional three-tier concept to support personalised treatment tailored to individual patients according to invasiveness, tolerability and effectiveness of the different therapeutic options and patients’ expectations. In this context, patients should be fully counselled with respect to all available treatment modalities.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Most men with ED are not treated with cause-specific therapeutic options. This results in a tailored treatment strategy that depends on invasiveness, efficacy, safety, costs and patient preference [370]. Physician-patient dialogue is essential throughout the management of ED. A systematic review has shown a consistent discontinuation rate for all available ED treatment options [371]. This highlights the importance of clinicians understanding patient’s beliefs about ED treatment, therapeutic ineffectiveness, adverse effects, quality of intimate relationships and treatment costs, all of which were shown to be the most prevalent barriers to treatment use [371].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>\u003Cstrong>Figure 5.5: Management algorithm for erectile dysfunction\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003Cimg src=\"https:\u002F\u002Fd56bochluxqnz.cloudfront.net\u002Fguideline-images\u002FSexual-Health-Guidelines-2026-Figure-5-5-Management-algorithm-for-erectile-dysfunction.jpg\" alt=\"\">\u003Ci>ED = erectile dysfunction; PDE5Is = phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors; Li-SWT = low-intensity shockwave therapy.\u003C\u002Fi>\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.1. Patient education\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Educational intervention is often the first approach to sexual complaints and consists of informing patients about the psychological and physiological processes involved in the individual’s sexual response in ways the patient can understand. This baseline approach has been shown to favour sexual satisfaction in men with ED [372]. Accordingly, consultation should include a discussion of the expectations and needs of the patient and their sexual partner. It should also review the patient’s and partner’s understanding of ED and the results of diagnostic tests, and provide a rationale for the selected treatment [370].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.2. Modifiable risk factors\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Erectile dysfunction may be associated with modifiable or reversible risk factors, including lifestyle or drug-related factors [373]. These factors may be modified either before, or at the same time as, specific therapies are used. Likewise, ED may be associated with concomitant and underlying conditions (e.g. endocrine and metabolic disorders, such as diabetes, and some CV problems, such as hypertension) which should always be well-controlled as the first step of any ED treatment [374]. Overall, several studies have shown that lifestyle modifications, including physical activity (particularly aerobic exercise), weight loss (including bariatric surgery), and treatment of CVD risk factors, may improve sexual function in men with ED [271,373,375-379]. Regarding the use of statins, previous meta-analyses have demonstrated an improvement in EF [380,381].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.3. Phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Four potent selective PDE5Is have been approved by the EMA for treatment of ED [382]. The efficacy of all four PD5EIs in almost every subgroup of patients with ED has been successfully established [382-385]. Efficacy is defined as an erection, with rigidity, sufficient for satisfactory intercourse [374]. Adverse events for the four PDE5Is are generally mild and self-limiting [386-390]. The pharmacokinetic data for all four PDE5Is and their associated adverse events are presented in Tables 5.3 and 5.4, respectively. Choice of PDE5I depends on frequency of intercourse and the patient’s personal experience. Two meta-analyses demonstrated that ED patients who prioritise high efficacy should use sildenafil 50mg whereas those who optimise tolerability should initially use tadalafil 10mg [383,391]. A meta-analysis investigating the placebo responses in patients treated with PDE5Is showed a significant improvement in ED, especially among men with ED-related post-traumatic stress disorder [392].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.3.a. Sildenafil\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>Sildenafil is administered in doses of 25, 50 and 100mg. The recommended starting dose is 50mg and should be adapted according to the patient’s response and adverse effects [393]. The window of effectiveness ranges from 30-60 minutes after administration [393] up to 12 hours [394]. In a 24-week dose-response study, improved erections were reported by 56%, 77% and 84% of general ED patients taking 25, 50 and 100mg sildenafil, respectively, compared to 25% of men taking placebo [395]. Sildenafil also significantly improved patient scores for IIEF, Sexual Encounter Profile (SEP) question 2 (SEP2), SEP question 3 (SEP3), General Assessment Questionnaire (GAQ), and treatment satisfaction [395].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>An orally disintegrating tablet (ODT) of sildenafil citrate at the dose of 25, 50, 75 and 100mg has been developed, mainly for patients who have difficulty swallowing solid dosage forms [396]. Sildenafil oral suspension treatment has been developed, with clinically relevant results [397,398].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.3.b. Tadalafil\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>Tadalafil is administered in on-demand doses of 10 and 20mg or a daily dose of 5mg. The recommended on-demand starting dose is 10mg and should be adapted according to the patient’s response and adverse effects [399,400]. The window of effectiveness ranges from 30 minutes after administration (peak efficacy after approximately two hours) up to 36 hours [399]. In a 12-week dose-response study improved erections were reported by 67% and 81% of men with ED taking 10 and 20mg tadalafil, respectively, compared to 35% of men taking placebo [399]. Tadalafil has also shown a net clinical benefit in the short-term on ejaculatory and orgasmic functions in ED patients [401].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Data have also shown that 40% of men aged &gt; 45 years were combined responders for ED and LUTS\u002FBPH when treated with tadalafil 5mg once daily, with symptom improvement after 12 weeks [402]. Therefore, its use may be considered in patients with ED only, as well as in those with concomitant LUTS who wish to benefit from a single therapy [403].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.3.c. Vardenafil\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>Vardenafil is administered in on-demand doses of 5, 10 and 20mg. The recommended starting dose is 10mg and should be adapted according to the patient’s response and adverse effects [388]. Vardenafil is effective from 30 minutes after administration [404], with one out of three patients achieving satisfactory erections within 15 minutes of ingestion [405]. In a 12-week dose-response study, improved erections were reported by 66%, 76% and 80% of men with ED taking 5, 10 and 20mg vardenafil, respectively, compared with 30% of men taking placebo [388,406]. Vardenafil significantly improved patient scores for IIEF, SEP2, SEP3, GAQ, and treatment satisfaction. An ODT formulation of vardenafil has been released [406]. The efficacy of vardenafil ODT has been demonstrated in several RCTs and did not seem to differ from the regular formulation [407-409].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.3.d. Avanafil\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>Avanafil is administered in on-demand doses of 50, 100 and 200mg [389]. The recommended starting dose is 100mg taken as needed 15-30 minutes before sexual activity and the dose may be adapted according to efficacy and tolerability [389,390,410]. In a general ED population the mean percentage of successful sexual attempts resulting in intercourse were 47%, 58% and 59% for the 50, 100 and 200mg groups, respectively, as compared with 28% for the placebo group [389,390]. A meta-analysis confirmed that avanafil had comparable efficacy with sildenafil, vardenafil and tadalafil [411].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.3.e. Continuous use of PDE5Is\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>According to the EMA, a once-daily regimen with tadalafil 2.5 or 5mg may be considered suitable, based on patients’ choice and physicians’ judgement. In these patients, the recommended dose is 5mg, taken once daily at approximately the same time each day. Tadalafil, 5mg once daily, provides an alternative to on-demand tadalafil for couples who prefer spontaneous rather than scheduled sexual activities or who anticipate frequent sexual activity, with the advantage that dosing and sexual activity no longer need to be linked. Regardless of the ED population, there is no clinically significant difference between continuous (once daily) and on-demand tadalafil treatment [412]. Overall, treatment with tadalafil 5mg once daily in men complaining of ED of various severities is well-tolerated and effective [413] and may improve EF in men who have a partial response to on-demand PDE5I therapy [414]. The appropriateness of the continuous use of a daily regimen should be reassessed periodically [413,415].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>\u003Cstrong>Table 5.3: Pharmacokinetics data for PDE5Is EMA approved for the treatment of ED*\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cfigure class=\"table\">\u003Ctable>\u003Ctbody>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Parameter\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Sildenafil, 100mg\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Tadalafil, 20mg\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Vardenafil, 20mg\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Avanafil, 200mg\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>C\u003Csub>max\u003C\u002Fsub>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>560μg\u002FL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>378μg\u002FL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>18.7μg\u002FL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>5.2μg\u002FL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>T\u003Csub>max\u003C\u002Fsub> (median)\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>0.8-1 hours\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>2 hours\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>0.9 hours\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>0.5-0.75 hours\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>T1\u002F2\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>2.6-3.7 hours\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>17.5 hours\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>3.9 hours\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>6-17 hours\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>AUC\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1,685μg.h\u002FL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>8,066μg.h\u002FL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>56.8μg.h\u002FL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>11.6μg.h\u002FL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Protein binding\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>96%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>94%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>94%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>99%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Bioavailability\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>41%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>NA\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>15%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>8-10%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003C\u002Ftbody>\u003C\u002Ftable>\u003C\u002Ffigure>\u003Cp>\u003Ci>* Fasted state, higher recommended dose. Data adapted from EMA statements on product characteristics. AUC = area under curve or serum concentration time curve; ED = erectile dysfunction; EMA = European Medicines Agency; C\u003Csub>max\u003C\u002Fsub> = maximal concentration; T\u003Csub>max\u003C\u002Fsub> = time-to-maximum plasma concentration; T1\u002F2 = plasma elimination halftime; PDE5Is = phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors.\u003C\u002Fi>\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>\u003Cstrong>Table 5.4: Common adverse events of the four PDE5Is currently EMA-approved to treat ED*\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cfigure class=\"table\">\u003Ctable>\u003Ctbody>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Adverse event\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Sildenafil\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Tadalafil\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Vardenafil\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Avanafil, 200mg\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Headache\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>12.8%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>14.5%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>16%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>9.3%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Flushing\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>10.4%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>4.1%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>12%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>3.7%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Dyspepsia\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>4.6%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>12.3%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>4%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>uncommon\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Nasal congestion\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1.1%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>4.3%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>10%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1.9%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Dizziness\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1.2%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>2.3%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>2%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>0.6%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Abnormal vision\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1.9%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>&nbsp;\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>&lt; 2%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>None\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Back pain\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>&nbsp;\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>6.5%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>&nbsp;\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>&lt; 2%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Myalgia\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>&nbsp;\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>5.7%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>&nbsp;\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>&lt; 2%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003C\u002Ftbody>\u003C\u002Ftable>\u003C\u002Ffigure>\u003Cp>\u003Ci>* Adapted from EMA statements on product characteristics.\u003C\u002Fi>\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.3.f. Safety concerns for PDE5Is\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Ch6>5.6.3.f.1. Cardiovascular safety\u003C\u002Fh6>\u003Cp>No RCTs or open-label studies have demonstrated an increase in myocardial infarction (MI) rates in patients receiving PDE5Is. In contrast, an observational study demonstrated that in men with stable coronary artery disease, treatment with PDE5I is associated with lower risks of death, MI, heart failure, and revascularisation compared with alprostadil treatment [416]. None of the PDE5Is have an adverse effect on total exercise time or time-to-ischaemia during exercise testing in men with stable angina [382,417]. By consensus, this EAU Guidelines Panel have agreed to maintain the recommendations of the 3\u003Csup>rd\u003C\u002Fsup> Princeton Consensus Panel for the prescription of PDE5Is to patients with CVD or high CV risk [338-340].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch6>5.6.3.f.2. Contraindications for the concomitant use of organic nitrates and nicorandil\u003C\u002Fh6>\u003Cp>An absolute contraindication to PDE5Is is the concomitant use of any form of organic nitrate or nitric oxide donors, including recreational use of amyl nitrite or nitrate (poppers). Concomitant use results in cGMP accumulation and unpredictable falls in blood pressure and symptoms of hypotension [418-421]. Concurrent use of nicorandil and PDE5Is is contraindicated due to the potential of the nitric oxide donating properties of nicorandil to increase cGMP levels [422].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch6>5.6.3.f.3. Antihypertensive drugs\u003C\u002Fh6>\u003Cp>Co-administration of PDE5Is with antihypertensive agents may result in small additive decreases in blood pressure, which are usually minor [338]. In general, the adverse event profile of a PDE5I is not worsened by a background of antihypertensive medication, even when the patient is taking several antihypertensive agents [423-425].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch6>5.6.3.f.4. Interactions with α-blockers\u003C\u002Fh6>\u003Cp>Tadalafil 5mg is currently the only licensed drug for the treatment of both ED and LUTS, demonstrating overall good efficacy in relieving urinary symptoms and improving EF [403]. Treatment with tadalafil 5mg should be considered in patients suffering from mild to moderate LUTS associated with ED, either alone or in combination with α-blockers. Conversely, as both drugs are vasodilators, a certain degree of caution has been observed for combination therapy with PDE5Is and alpha-blockers due to the potential cumulative effects on blood pressure described in some studies [394,405,426]. A meta-analysis concluded that concomitant treatment with α-blockers (both non-uroselective [e.g. terazosin and doxazosin] and uro-selective [e.g. alfuzosin, tamsulosin and silodosin] and PDE5Is may produce changes in haemodynamic parameters, but it does not increase the rate of adverse events due to hypotension [426]. Therefore, there is no current limitation in the simultaneous use of α-blockers and PDE5I.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch6>5.6.3.f.5. Management of non- or poor-responders to PDE5Is\u003C\u002Fh6>\u003Cp>The management of non-responders depends upon identifying the underlying cause [427]. Clinicians should begin by ensuring that the medication has been properly prescribed, is being correctly used by the patient and that the patient has been using a licensed medication. Absorption of sildenafil, vardenafil and avanafil can be delayed by high-fat meals [428-430]. Timing is important, and patients may be waiting either too short or too long after the medication before attempting sexual intercourse. Studies suggest that patient education can help salvage an apparent non-responder to a PDE5I [427,431-434]. After emphasising the importance of dose, timing and sexual stimulation to the patient, EF can be effectively restored following re-administration of the relevant PDE5I [427,431,432].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Studies have demonstrated that patients with hypogonadism who do not respond to PDE5Is may improve their response to PDE5Is after initiating testosterone therapy [94,374,435]. Therefore, when diagnostic criteria suggestive of testosterone deficiency are met, testosterone therapy may be more appropriate, even in ED patients [3,94].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Limited data suggest that some patients might respond better to one PDE5I than to another [436], raising the possibility that, despite an identical mode of action, switching to a different PDE5I may be beneficial. However, no evidence for this has been reported in the available RCTs [437,438].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>In refractory, complex or difficult-to-treat cases of ED, a combination therapy should be considered as a first-line approach. Although the available data are still limited, there are several strategies that can be considered including: PDE5Is with antioxidant agents, LI-SWT or a vacuum erection device (VED) [439]; daily tadalafil with an on-demand short-acting PDE5I (such as sildenafil) [440]; Li-SWT with VED [441]; and PDE5Is with topical alprostadil [442].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.3.g. Topical\u002Fintraurethral alprostadil\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>The vasoactive agent alprostadil can be administered inside the urethra with two different formulations. The first delivery method is topical, using a cream that includes a permeation enhancer to facilitate absorption of alprostadil (200 and 300μg) via the urethral meatus [443,444]. Clinical data are still limited. Significant improvement compared to placebo was recorded for IIEF-EF domain score, SEP2, and SEP3 in a broad range of patients with mild-to-severe ED [445]. Adverse effects include penile erythema, penile burning, and pain that usually resolve within two hours of application. Topical alprostadil (VITAROSTM) at a dose of 300μg is available in some European countries. A randomised cross-over clinical trial has shown that, compared to the standard administration route, direct delivery within the urethral meatus can increase efficacy and confidence among patients, without increasing adverse effects [446].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>The second delivery method is by intra-urethral insertion of a specific formulation of alprostadil (125-1000μg) in a medicated pellet (MUSE™) [237]. Erections sufficient for intercourse are achieved in 30-65.9% of patients. In clinical practice, it is recommended that intra-urethral alprostadil is initiated at a dose of 500μg, as it has a higher efficacy than the 250μg dose, with minimal differences in adverse events. In case of unsatisfactory clinical response, the dose can be increased to 1000μg [447-449]. Overall, the most common adverse events are local pain (29-41%) and dizziness with possible hypotension (1.9-14%). Penile fibrosis and priapism are rare (&lt; 1%). Urethral bleeding (5%) and urinary tract infections (0.2%) are adverse events related to the mode of administration.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Efficacy rates are significantly lower than for intracavernous pharmacotherapy [450], with 30% adherence to long-term therapy. Intraurethral pharmacotherapy provides an alternative to intracavernous injections for patients who prefer a less invasive, but less efficacious treatment.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.4. Psychosocial intervention and therapy\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Psychosocial interventions, including different modalities (e.g. sexual skills training, marital therapy, psychosexual education) [372], and cognitive and behavioural therapy (CBT) - group or couple format - even if conducted online, are recommended [367,451]. By modifying maladaptive cognitive and behavioural patterns that contribute to ED, CBT enhances psychological adjustment throughout the course of the disorder. The integration of CBT with pharmacological treatment for ED has demonstrated empirical efficacy and is regarded as a best-practice approach [452].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.5. Hormonal treatment\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>When clinically indicated, testosterone therapy (intramuscular, transdermal or oral) can be considered for men with low or low-normal testosterone levels and concomitant problems with sexual desire, EF and dissatisfaction derived from intercourse and overall sex life (see Section 3.4 for a comprehensive discussion on testosterone therapy) [453,454], even in patients with a history of CVD [455].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.6. Vacuum erection devices\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Published data report that efficacy, in terms of erections satisfactory for intercourse, is as high as 90%, regardless of the cause of ED, and satisfaction rates range between 27% and 94% [456,457]. Long-term use of VEDs decreases to 50-64% after two years [458]. The most common adverse events include pain, inability to ejaculate, petechiae, bruising and numbness [457]. Serious adverse events (skin necrosis) can be avoided if patients remove the constriction ring within 30 minutes. In patients with bleeding disorders or on anticoagulant therapy, VEDs are contraindicated [459,460]. For well-informed older patients with infrequent sexual intercourse and comorbidity who require non-invasive, drug-free management of ED, VEDs may be the treatment of choice [456,457,461].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.7. Intracavernous injections therapy\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Intracavernous administration of vasoactive drugs was the first medical treatment introduced for ED [434,462]. Patients may be offered intracavernous injections at every stage of a tailored treatment work-up.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.7.a. Alprostadil\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>Alprostadil (CaverjectTM, Edex\u002FViridalTM) was the first and only drug approved for intracavernous treatment of ED [434,463]. Intracavernous alprostadil is most efficacious as a monotherapy at a dose of 5-40μg (40μg may be offered off label in some European countries). The erection appears after 5-15 minutes and lasts according to the dose injected, but with significant heterogeneity among patients. An in-office training programme is required for patients to learn the injection technique. Efficacy rates for intracavernous alprostadil of &gt; 70% have been found in the general ED population, as well as in patient subgroups (e.g. men with diabetes or CVD), with reported satisfaction rates of 87-93.5% in patients and 86-90.3% in partners after the injections [434,462]. Complications of intracavernous alprostadil include penile pain (50% of patients reported pain only after 11% of total injections), excessively prolonged and undesired erections (5%), priapism (1%), and fibrosis (2%) [434,462,464]. Pain is usually self-limited after prolonged use and it can be alleviated with the addition of sodium bicarbonate or local anaesthesia [434,462,465]. Cavernosal fibrosis usually clears within a few months after temporary discontinuation of the injection programme. However, tunical fibrosis suggests early onset of PD and may indicate the need to discontinue intracavernous injections indefinitely. Systemic adverse effects are uncommon. The most common is mild hypotension, especially when using higher doses. In a large population study including 16,548 men with a history of ischemic CVD, those treated with alprostadil had higher risk of a further CV event as compared to those receiving PDE5Is [416]. Contraindications include men with a history of hypersensitivity to alprostadil, men at risk of priapism, and men with bleeding disorders. Despite these favourable data, drop-out rates of 41-68% have been reported for intracavernous pharmacotherapy [434,462,466,467], with most discontinuations occurring within the first two to three months. Careful counselling of patients during the office-training phase as well as close follow-up are important in addressing patient withdrawal from an intracavernous injection programme [468-470].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.7.b. Other vasoactive intracavernous treatments\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>Table 5.5 details the available intracavernous injection therapies (compounds and characteristics). Combination therapy enables a patient to take advantage of the different modes of action of the drugs being used, as well as alleviating adverse effects by using lower doses of each drug.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Papaverine (20-80mg) was the first oral drug used for intracavernous injections. It is most commonly used in combination therapy because of its high incidence of adverse effects as monotherapy. Papaverine is currently not licensed for treatment of ED.\u003C\u002Fli>\u003Cli>Phentolamine has been used in combination therapy to increase efficacy. As monotherapy, it produces a poor erectile response. Phentolamine is currently not licensed for treatment of ED.\u003C\u002Fli>\u003Cli>Limited data support the use of other drugs, such as vasoactive intestinal peptide, nitric oxide donors (linsidomine), forskolin, potassium channel openers, moxisylyte or calcitonin gene-related peptide, usually combined with the main drugs [471,472]. Most combinations are not standardised, and some drugs have limited availability worldwide.\u003C\u002Fli>\u003Cli>Bimix, (papaverine 7.5-45mg plus phentolamine 0.25-1.5mg) and Trimix (papaverine 8-16mg plus phentolamine 0.2-0.4mg plus alprostadil 10-20μg), have been widely used with improved efficacy rates, although they have never been licensed for ED [473,474]. Trimix has the highest efficacy rates, reaching 92%; this combination has similar adverse effects as alprostadil monotherapy, but a lower incidence of penile pain due to lower doses of alprostadil. However, fibrosis is more common (5-10%) when papaverine is used (depending on total dose).\u003C\u002Fli>\u003Cli>InvicorpTM: Vasoactive intestinal peptide (25μg) plus phentolamine mesylate (1-2mg Invicorp), is a combination of two active components with complementary modes of action. Clinical studies have shown that the combination is effective for intracavernous injections in &gt; 80% of men with ED, including those who have failed to respond to other therapies and, unlike existing intracavernous therapies, is associated with a low incidence of penile pain and a virtually negligible risk of priapism [475].\u003C\u002Fli>\u003C\u002Ful>\u003Cp>Overall, despite high efficacy rates, 5-10% of patients do not respond to combination intracavernous injections.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>\u003Cstrong>Table 5.5: Intracavernous injection therapy - compounds and characteristics\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cfigure class=\"table\">\u003Ctable>\u003Ctbody>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Name\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Substance\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Dosage\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Efficacy\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Adverse Events\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Comment\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Caverject™ or Edex\u002FViridal™\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Alprostadil\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>5-40µg\u002FmL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>~ 70%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Penile pain, priapism, fibrosis\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Easily available\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Papaverine\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Papaverine\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>20-80mg\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>&lt; 55%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Elevation of liver enzymes, priapism, fibrosis\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Abandoned as monotherapy\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Phentolamine\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Phentolamine\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>0.5mg\u002FmL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Poor efficacy as monotherapy\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Systemic hypotension, reflex tachycardia, nasal congestion, and gastrointestinal upset\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Abandoned as monotherapy\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Bimix\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Papaverine + Phentolamine\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>30mg\u002FmL +\u003Cbr>0.5mg\u002FmL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>~ 90%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Similar to Alprostadil (less pain)\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Not licensed for the treatment of ED\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Trimix\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Papaverine + Phentolamine + Alprostadil\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>30mg\u002FmL +\u003Cbr>1mg\u002FmL +\u003Cbr>10µg\u002FmL\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>~ 92%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Similar to Alprostadil (less pain)\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Not licensed for the treatment of ED\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Invicorp™\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Vasoactive intestinal peptide+ Phentolamine\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>25µg + 1-2mg\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>~ 80%\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Similar as Alprostadil without pain\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Easily available\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003C\u002Ftbody>\u003C\u002Ftable>\u003C\u002Ffigure>\u003Ch4>5.6.8. Innovative treatment modalities\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>There are currently several potential novel treatment modalities for ED. Most of these therapeutic approaches require further investigation in large-scale, blinded, placebo-controlled randomised studies to achieve adequate evidence-based and clinically reliable recommendation grades [476-481].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.8.a. Regenerative medicine therapies\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Ch6>5.6.8.a.1. Shockwave therapy\u003C\u002Fh6>\u003Cp>The use of LI-SWT has been increasingly proposed as a treatment for vasculogenic ED over the last decade, and it’s the only currently marketed treatment that might offer a cure, which is the most desired outcome for most men suffering from ED [351,482-489].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Several single-arm trials have shown a beneficial effect of LI-SWT on patient-reported EF. However, there are significant differences in reported outcome measures due to the heterogeneity among shockwave generators, type of shockwaves delivered (focal vs. radical), set-up parameters and treatment protocols [490,491]. In a trial trying to assess the best treatment parameters, no significant differences were observed between various energy flux density levels; although, a 0.10mJ\u002Fmm\u003Csup>2\u003C\u002Fsup> seems to perform slightly better than lower energies [492]. Most trials have been conducted with generators delivering focal shockwaves [351,490,491].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>A RCT using radial wave therapy showed no difference in IIEF-EF and EHS score between treatment and placebo groups [493]. Despite this, most studies have suggested that LI-SWT can significantly increase IIEF and EHS scores in patients with mild vasculogenic ED, although this improvement appears modest and the rates of patients reporting a satisfactory improvement range between 40-80% [351,490]. Few studies have shown an improvement in penile haemodynamic parameters after LI-SWT, but the clinical meaning of this improvement remains unclear [490,494]. Likewise, data suggest that LI-SWT could ameliorate erection quality even in patients with severe ED who are either PDE5Is non-responders [487,495,496] or inadequate responders [497], thus reducing the immediate need for more invasive treatments. Treatment effect appears to be clinically evident starting from one to three months after treatment completion, with a subsequent progressive decrease of the achieved EF benefit over time, although some effects could be still detected up to five years after treatment [490,492,498]. Data from RCTs suggest that even better results could be achieved by combining LI-SWT with other treatments such as a VED in men with T2DM [441] or daily tadalafil [499,500].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Findings from a meta-analysis showed that LI-ESWT has a positive effect on early recovery of EF in the context of penile rehabilitation of ED after RP. However, the level of evidence was low and careful interpretation of the results is required [501-503].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch6>5.6.8.a.2. Platelet-rich plasma\u003C\u002Fh6>\u003Cp>Intracavernous injection of platelet-rich plasma (PRP) has been investigated in several prospective and retrospective trials [504-510]. The regenerative effect of PRP is deemed to be exerted through the high concentrations of platelets containing several growth factors including VEGF, EGF, IGF-1, PDGF and FGF [511]. These factors may be responsible for angiogenesis stimulation and stem cell recruitment [511].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>An RCT investigated the effect of intracavernous injection of PRP for ED. Sixty patients with mild to moderate vasculogenic ED were randomised to receive two injections of 10mL PRP (n = 30) or placebo (n = 30) [510]. At one, three and six-month follow-up, the rate of patients reporting minimal clinically important difference (MCID) in the IIEF-EF score was significantly higher in the treatment group, with 69% achieving MCID six months after PRP versus 27% in the placebo group (p &lt; 0.001). The IIEF-EF scores improved by a mean of 2.7 points at one-month and 3.9 points at six-month assessment after treatment. Regarding safety, no haemorrhagic events or other side effects were reported [510].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>A prospective randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled study was carried out on 109 patients, aged 45-65 years, with mild to moderate ED, following cessation of any ED treatment [512]. At one, three and six months after PRP injections, patients in the PRP group had a significant improvement compared to placebo in terms of IIEF-EF, SEP2 and SEP3 scores. Moreover, at six-month post-treatment follow-up, 70% of patients achieved a MCID in the PRP group compared to 16% in the placebo group [512]. A 2023 prospective, randomised, double-blind, placebo control study on a relatively small cohort of mild to moderate ED patients treated with two PRP injections separated by one month, showed that the treatment is safe, but no difference in efficacy between PRP and placebo was found [513]. Of clinical relevance, patients were allowed to continue PDE5Is during the study [513]. A meta-analysis of the above mentioned RCTs reported a mean improvement in IIEF-EF score with PRP versus placebo of 3.21 (95% CI: 1.82; 4.60) six months after treatment [514].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Despite promising results on the efficacy and safety of PRP treatment for primary organic ED, current evidence remains insufficient to provide a recommendation for its use in clinical practice [515]. There is heterogeneity among studies in terms of timing and dosing regimens, with no consensus on the optimal activation method and platelet concentration for each PRP injection, and the need to measure qualitative and quantitative composition of growth factors and cytokines [515,516]. Intracavernous injection of PRP should be used only in a clinical trial setting, as larger trials are needed to confirm findings and define the efficacy and safety of PRP for treatment of ED.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch6>5.6.8.a.3. Stem-cells\u003C\u002Fh6>\u003Cp>The use of stem cells as a regenerative treatment for ED is under investigation. A systematic review summarising the results of 18 phase I and II clinical trials reported a large heterogeneity in terms of the type of stem cells used, treatment protocols and patients’ characteristics [517]. The overall number of included patients was 373 and the results confirmed the safety of intracavernous injection of stem cells. No definitive conclusions can be drawn in terms of efficacy, given that only four small trials were placebo-controlled and their findings were conflicting. To date, data are still insufficient to provide a clinical recommendation.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.8.b. Botulinum neurotoxin\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>Botulinum Neurotoxin A (BoNT-A) has been investigated as a possible ED treatment [518]. Two RCTs have investigated the effect of BoNT-A for the treatment of patients with ED who were non-responders to PDE5Is or intracavernous injection of pro-erectile drugs [519,520]. One trial randomised 70 patients with ED refractory to PDE5Is to receive a single intracavernous injection of 100UI of BoNT-A or saline [519]. Patients in both groups were instructed to keep using on-demand high-dose PDE5Is. The RCT showed an improvement in EHS and PSV at two weeks post-treatment. At six weeks, the treatment group showed a 5 point improvement in the SHIM score versus no improvement in the placebo group, with 53% of patients reporting an erection hard enough for vaginal penetration [519]. The second trial randomised 176 patients, all non-responders to PDE5Is or intracavernous injection trimix, to three treatment groups: BoNT-A 100UI; BoNT-A 50UI; or placebo [520]. A significant improvement in SHIM, EHS and SEP scores was reported in both treatment groups, with a maximum response rate reached three months after treatment. Overall, the RCT showed that up to 40% of patients were able to resume satisfactory sexual activity after treatment [520]. Both trials reported only mild local side effects with no systemic complications.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Other single-arm, non-controlled studies have confirmed these findings [521,522], showing a promising role for BoNT-A in the treatment of patients who are non-responders to well-established ED therapies. At present, no recommendation for its use in clinical practice can be provided, as larger trials are needed to confirm findings and define the efficacy and safety of BoNT-A for treatment of ED.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.9. Herbal medicine and natural supplements\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>There has been an exponential growth in the market of medicinal herbs and natural supplements for the treatment of ED, but with very little available evidence of robust scientific data to support their efficacy and safety. A Cochrane review showed that ginseng may only have trivial effects on EF or satisfaction with intercourse compared to placebo when assessed using validated tools [523]. A meta-analysis including 18 trials comparing the effect of antioxidants alone or in combination with PDE5Is versus placebo or PDE5Is alone showed a small but significant positive effect of L-arginine with a mean increase in IIEF-EF score of 2.74 (95% CI: 1.67; 3.81) compared to placebo, but no difference in terms of sexual satisfaction score [524]. Similar results were reported for antioxidants alone including folic acid, myoinositol, vitamin E, L-carnitine, nicotinic acid, pine bark extract, transresveratrol, L-citrulline and ginseng. This small improvement may not be perceived as significant by patients. In another meta-analysis, the combination of PDE5Is plus antioxidants (including: propionyl-L-carnitine, acetyl-L-carnitine, L-arginine, folic acid and trans-resveratrol) was associated with better EF improvement compared to PDE5Is alone [439]. Overall, these data suggest that antioxidant supplements and herbal compounds alone or in combination with PDE5Is may have a small positive effect on EF, although further studies are needed to assess the clinical significance of these findings.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.10. Erectile dysfunction after radical prostatectomy\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Urologists should have enhanced awareness of how to approach patients directly about their ED and sexual dysfunction following RP and actively offer treatment options [525]. Use of pro-erectile drugs following RP is important in achieving postoperative EF and to allow patients to resume sexual activity. Several trials have shown improvements in EF after RP in patients receiving drugs (any therapeutic or prophylactic) for ED. Early, compared with delayed, EF treatment affects the natural recovery time for EF [526], although there is limited data to support any specific regimen, which is either optimal for penile rehabilitation or may result in the achievement of spontaneous, non-pharmacologically assisted erections [304,527,528]. Data from a small pilot RCT showed that perioperative rehabilitation with PDE5Is may lead to better EF recovery compared to postoperative rehabilitation [529]. There is no evidence that penile rehabilitation itself increases the chances of spontaneous EF recovery in men following nerve-sparing RP [528]. The currently available therapeutic armamentarium follows the treatment algorithm for ED, which is shown in Figure 5.2.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>In this context, PDE5Is have been considered as the first-line therapy in patients who have undergone nerve-sparing surgery, regardless of the surgical technique used [304,309]. Several clinical parameters have been identified as potential predictors of PDE5Is outcomes in men undergoing RP. Patient age, baseline EF and quality of nerve-sparing technique are key factors in preserving post-RP EF [309,312,530].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>A Cochrane review analysing data from eight RCTs showed that scheduled PDE5Is may have little or no effect on short-term (up to 12 months) self-reported potency when compared to placebo or no treatment [531]. In this study, a daily PDE5I made little to no difference in short- and long-term EF. It was concluded that penile rehabilitation strategies using PDE5I following RP do not increase self-reported EF compared to on-demand use. Conversely, an updated meta-analysis including 22 trials comparing 16 different rehabilitation protocols, showed that daily administration of sildenafil 100mg was the most effective strategy to recover EF after RP [532].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Intracavernous injections and penile implants had been suggested as second- and third-line treatments, respectively, when oral PDE5Is are not adequately effective or not suitable for postoperative patients [304,533]. A meta-analysis showed that the early use of VED has an excellent therapeutic effect on post-RP patients and no serious adverse effects; therefore, it should be considered as a therapeutic alternative [534]. Findings from two network meta-analyses showed that combination therapy with VED and PDE5Is offers clear advantages over monotherapy, even in post-RP patients. This combined approach should be considered in the clinical management of ED after RP [535]. A novel penile traction device yielded positive results compared to no treatment in preserving penile length after RP [536].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Findings from a systematic review had suggested that pelvic floor muscle training combined with bio-feedback is a promising alternative to pharmacological treatments, although there is a need for future well-powered, rigorously designed RCTs to draw strong conclusions [537].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.11. Surgical management\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Ch5>5.6.11.a. Surgery for post-traumatic arteriogenic ED\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>In young patients with pelvic or perineal trauma, surgical penile revascularisation has a 60-70% long-term success rate [460,538]. The stenosis must be confirmed by penile pharmaco-arteriography. Corporeal veno-occlusive dysfunction is a contraindication to revascularisation and must be excluded by dynamic infusion cavernosometry or cavernosography.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.11.b. Venous ligation surgery\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>Venous ligation surgery for veno-occlusive dysfunction is no longer recommended because of poor long-term results [538].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>5.6.11.c. Penile prostheses\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>The surgical implantation of a penile prosthesis may be considered in patients who i) are not suitable for different pharmacotherapies or prefer a definitive therapy; and, ii) do not respond to other treatment modalities [539].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>The two currently available classes of penile implants include inflatable (two- and three-piece) and semi-rigid devices (malleable, mechanical and soft flexible) [309,540-543]. There are currently no head-to-head studies comparing the different manufacturers’ implants or demonstrating superiority of one implant type over another [544]. Patients may prefer the three-piece inflatable devices due to the more “natural” erections obtained, although no prospective RCTs have compared satisfaction rates with both types of implants. The two-piece inflatable prosthesis can be a viable option for patients who are deemed at high-risk of complications with reservoir placements (e.g. previous abdominal surgery). Semi-rigid prostheses result in a firm penis, which may be manually placed in an erect or flaccid state and offer the advantage of a simple implant technique, as well as easy use for the patient [309,540-542]. Conversely, they can have the disadvantage of unnatural persistent erection and reduced concealability [542,545]. They may also be an option in men with limited manual dexterity.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>There are two main surgical approaches for penile prosthesis implantation (PPI): peno-scrotal and infrapubic [541,542,545,546]. A systematic review comparing the satisfaction and complication rates of the different surgical approaches has shown that there is no specific advantage between the two, but rather it is recommended that surgeons have knowledge of both techniques and are capable of tailoring the incision strategy for complex cases [547]. Regardless of the indication, prosthesis implantation has one of the highest satisfaction rates (92-100% in patients and 91-95% in partners) among the treatment options for ED, with appropriate counselling [309,540,541,548-556]. Focused psychosexual counselling may improve sexuality and sexual well-being in both patients and their partners after penile implant surgery [557]. There is sufficient evidence to recommend this approach in patients who do not respond to less-invasive treatments due to its high efficacy, safety and satisfaction rate [558].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>The two main complications of PPI are mechanical failure and infection. Several technical modifications of the most commonly used three-piece prostheses (e.g. AMS 700CX\u002FCXRTM and Titan Zero degree™) resulted in mechanical failure rates of &lt; 5% after five years of follow-up [540,559,560]. A meta-analysis showed implant durability or survival rates of 93.3% at one year, 91.0% at three years, 87.2% at five years, 76.8% at ten years, 63.7% at 15 years, and 52.9% at 20 years [561]. Careful surgical techniques with appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis against Gram-positive and negative bacteria reduced infection rates to 2-3% with primary implantation in low-risk patients and in high-volume centres [562-565]. The infection rate may be further reduced to 1-2% by implanting an antibiotic-impregnated prosthesis (AMS Inhibizone™) or hydrophilic-coated prosthesis (Coloplast Titan™) [540,562,566-569]. Methods that appear to decrease infection rates include using coated prostheses and strictly adhering to surgical techniques and protocols that avoid prolonged wound exposure and minimise skin contact (i.e. no-touch technique). Techniques that might prevent penile prostheses infection but lack definitive evidence include the use of prolonged postoperative antibiotics (&gt; 24 hours), shaving with clippers, and preparation with chlorhexidine-alcohol [570,571]. Identification and pre-treatment of patients who are colonised with nasal Staphylococcus aureus with mupirocin and chlorhexidine prior to surgery has been shown to reduce the incidence of postoperative surgical site infection from 4.4% to 0.9% [572].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>A large database-study has shown that diabetes mellitus is a risk factor for penile prostheses infection, highlighting the need for optimal patient selection [573]. There are no RCTs determining the ideal and\u002For correct threshold of glycated haemoglobin that is acceptable prior to implant surgery in diabetic patients [574]. A large-cohort, multicentre, retrospective analysis in men with diabetes who received a Coloplast Titan™ implant demonstrated that vancomycin plus gentamicin was the most efficacious combination of antibiotics used for implant dipping in terms of preventing postoperative infection and subsequent explantation and revision [575]. A retrospective analysis of a large USA insurance claim database showed that hypogonadism was independently associated with infection of the implant [576]. Patients with spinal cord injury had higher risk of complications, with up to 16% of cases reporting prosthesis infection in published series [577].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Prosthetic infection requires removal of the prosthesis and antibiotic administration. Alternatively, removal of the infected device followed by immediate salvage and replacement with a new prosthesis using a wash-out protocol has achieved successful salvages in &gt; 80% of cases [563,578-580]. No recommendation on how to proceed after explantation can be made, and the decision must be based on a detailed discussion of the pros and cons of salvage therapy after full consultation with the patient.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Besides infection and mechanical failure, impending implant erosion involving the distal corpora, urethra, or glans can occur in 1-6% of cases after surgery [581]. Moreover, non-infectious reservoir complications including injury to pelvic structures such as bladder, bowel and blood vessels have been described [582]. Similarly, glans ischaemia and necrosis have been reported in about 1.5% of patients [581,583]. Risk factors for these serious complications are higher in those patients with significant vascular impairment, such as patients with diabetes or who have undergone concomitant lengthening procedures.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>For penile prostheses models available on the market please see Appendix 3 online supplementary evidence.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>5.6.12. Summary of evidence and recommendations for treatment of ED\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cfigure class=\"table\">\u003Ctable>\u003Ctbody>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Summary of evidence\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>LE\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Lifestyle changes can lead to ED improvement in specific populations.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1a\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>PDE5Is are associated with significant improvement of EF with a good overall safety profile.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1a\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>There are no demonstrated differences among different PDE5Is in terms of treatment efficacy.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1a\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Topical\u002Fintraurethral alprostadil is effective in improving EF but data are still limited.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1b\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Vacuum therapy can improve EF with a wide range of treatment satisfaction rate.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>2b\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Intracavernous injection with alprostadil is an effective treatment of ED; however, it has relatively high treatment drop-out rates.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1a\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Low-intensity shockwave therapy can induce a mild improvement in EF among patients with vasculogenic ED.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1a\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Intracavernous injections of PRP have led to a mild improvement of EF among patients with organic ED, but the available evidence remains insufficient to provide a recommendation regarding its use.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1b\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>BoNT-A has been shown to improve response rate to medical treatment for ED in patients who were non-responsive to oral or injective therapies, but data are still limited.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1b\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Penile rehabilitation with PDE5Is after RP does not increase the chance of spontaneous EF recovery.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>1a\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>There is no difference in terms of efficacy and safety among different penile implants available or surgical approach used.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>3\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003C\u002Ftbody>\u003C\u002Ftable>\u003C\u002Ffigure>\u003Cfigure class=\"table\">\u003Ctable>\u003Ctbody>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Recommendations\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Strength rating\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Initiate lifestyle changes and risk factor modification prior to, or at the same time as, initiating erectile dysfunction (ED) treatments.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Use phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors (PDE5Is) as first-line therapy for the treatment of ED.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Use intracavernous injections as an alternative first-line therapy in well-informed patients or as second-line therapy.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cp>Use topical\u002Fintra-urethral alprostadil as an alternative first-line therapy in well-informed patients who:\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>do not wish to have or are not suitable for oral vasoactive therapy;\u003C\u002Fli>\u003Cli>do not wish to have intracavernous injections;\u003C\u002Fli>\u003Cli>prefer a less-invasive therapy.\u003C\u002Fli>\u003C\u002Ful>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Weak\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Use vacuum erection devices in well-informed patients requesting non-invasive, drug-free management of ED.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Weak\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cp>Use low intensity shockwave treatment with\u002Fwithout PDE5Is in patients:\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>with mild vasculogenic ED;\u003C\u002Fli>\u003Cli>as an alternative therapy in well-informed patients who do not wish to have or are not suitable for oral vasoactive therapy;\u003C\u002Fli>\u003Cli>who are vasculogenic ED patients that are poor responders to PDE5Is.\u003C\u002Fli>\u003C\u002Ful>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Weak\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Use supplements with L-arginine or ginseng daily in men with mild ED who refuse pharmacological treatment after counselling them that the improvement of EF could be mild.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Weak\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Direct the patient to cognitive behaviour therapy as a psychological approach (include the partner), when indicated, combined with medical treatment to maximise treatment outcomes.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Fully inform patients of the mechanism of action and the ways in which PDE5Is should be taken, as incorrect use\u002Finadequate information is the main causes of a lack of response to PDE5Is.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Weak\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Implant a penile prosthesis if other treatments fail or depending upon patient preference. Patients should be fully informed of the benefits and harms associated with the procedure.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Discuss with patients undergoing active treatment for prostate cancer (PCa) about the risk of sexual changes other than ED, including sexual desire reduction, changes in orgasm, anejaculation, Peyronie’s-like disease and penile size changes.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Strong\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Inform patients that available data are inadequate to support any specific regimen for penile rehabilitation.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Weak\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Start pro-erectile treatments at the earliest opportunity after radical prostatectomy\u002Fpelvic surgery and other curative treatments for PCa.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>Weak\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003C\u002Ftbody>\u003C\u002Ftable>\u003C\u002Ffigure>\u003Ch3>5.7. Follow-up\u003C\u002Fh3>\u003Cp>Follow-up is important to assess efficacy and safety of the treatment provided. It is also essential to assess patient satisfaction, as successful treatment for ED goes beyond efficacy and safety. Physicians must recognise that no single treatment is suitable for all patients or clinical situations, as detailed in the previous sections.\u003C\u002Fp>",{"title":1871,"description":1814,"social":1877,"advanced":1883},{"facebook":1878,"twitter":1881},{"title":1879,"description":1814,"image":1880},"MANAGEMENT OF ERECTILE DYSFUNCTION - Uroweb",{"url":1819},{"title":1879,"description":1814,"image":1882},{"url":1822},{"robots":1884,"canonical":1814},[],{"title":1886,"id":1887,"uri":1888,"slug":1889,"chapterContent":1890,"seo":1891},"DISORDERS OF EJACULATION","2130483","guidelines\u002Fsexual-and-reproductive-health\u002Fchapter\u002Fdisorders-of-ejaculation","disorders-of-ejaculation","\u003Ch2>6. DISORDERS OF EJACULATION\u003C\u002Fh2>\u003Ch3>6.1. Introduction\u003C\u002Fh3>\u003Cp>Ejaculation is a complex physiological process that comprises emission and expulsion processes and is mediated by interwoven neurological, hormonal and anatomical pathways [584,585]. Any interference with those pathways may cause a wide range of ejaculatory disorders. The spectrum of ejaculation disorders includes PE, retarded or DE, anejaculation, painful ejaculation, RE, anorgasmia and haemospermia.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch3>6.2. Premature ejaculation\u003C\u002Fh3>\u003Ch4>6.2.1. Epidemiology\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Historically, the main problem in assessing the prevalence of PE has been the lack of a universally recognised definition at the time that surveys were conducted [586]. See Section 4.2 for a comprehensive discussion of the epidemiology of PE.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>6.2.2. Pathophysiology and risk factors\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>The aetiology of PE is relatively unknown, with limited data to support suggested biological and psychological hypotheses, including anxiety [587-590], penile hypersensitivity [591-598] and 5-hydroxytryptamine (HT) receptor dysfunction [599-604]. The classification of PE into four subtypes [214] has contributed to a better delineation of lifelong, acquired, variable and subjective PE [605-607]. It has been hypothesised that the pathophysiology of lifelong PE is mediated by a complex interplay of central and peripheral serotonergic, dopaminergic, oxytocinergic, endocrinological, genetic and epigenetic factors [608]. Acquired PE may occur due to psychological problems, such as sexual performance anxiety, psychological or relationship problems, and\u002For comorbidity, including ED, prostatitis, hyperthyroidism and poor sleep quality [609-612]. Variable PE is considered to be a normal variation of sexual function, whereas subjective PE can stem from cultural or abnormal psychological constructs [214].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>A significant proportion of males with ED also experience PE [218,613-616]. High levels of performance anxiety related to ED may worsen PE, with a risk of misdiagnosing PE instead of the underlying ED. According to the National Health and Social Life Survey (NHSLS), the prevalence of PE is not affected by age [210], unlike ED which increases with age. Premature ejaculation is not affected by marital or income status [210,617]. However, PE is more common in black and hispanic males, as well as males from regions where an Islamic background is common [210,618-621]. The prevalence may be higher in males with a lower educational level [210,218]. Other reported risk factors for PE include genetic predisposition [604,622-625], poor overall health status and obesity [210], prostate inflammation [626-630], hyperthyroidism [609], low prolactin levels [631], high testosterone levels [632], vitamin D and B12 deficiency [633,634], diabetes [635,636], MetS [637,638], lack of physical activity [639], emotional problems and stress [210,640,641], depressive symptoms [641], and traumatic sexual experiences [210,218].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>6.2.3. Impact of PE on quality of life\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Men with PE are more likely to report low satisfaction with their sexual relationship, low satisfaction with sexual intercourse, difficulty relaxing during intercourse, and less frequent intercourse [642-644]. Premature ejaculation can have a detrimental effect on self-confidence and the relationship with a partner, and may sometimes cause mental distress, anxiety, embarrassment and depression [642,645,646]. Premature ejaculation may also affect the partner’s sexual functioning, and their satisfaction with the sexual relationship decreases with increasing severity of the patient’s condition [647-649]. Despite the possible serious psychological and QoL consequences of PE, few men seek treatment [211,218,615,650-652].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch4>6.2.4. Classification\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>There is still little consensus about the definition and classification of PE [653]. It is now universally accepted that 'premature ejaculation' is a broad term that includes several concepts belonging to the common category of PE. The International Classification of Diseases 11\u003Csup>th\u003C\u002Fsup> Revision (ICD-11) renamed PE as Early Ejaculation [654], with the following definition: \u003Ci>“Male early ejaculation is characterized by ejaculation that occurs prior to or within a very short duration of the initiation of vaginal penetration or other relevant sexual stimulation, with no or little perceived control over ejaculation. The pattern of early ejaculation has occurred episodically or persistently over a period of at least several months and is associated with clinically significant distress.”\u003C\u002Fi>\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>This definition includes four categories: male early ejaculation; lifelong generalised and situational; acquired generalised and situational; and unspecified. The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders V (DSM-V) [220] and the International Society for Sexual Medicine (ISSM) [655] published definitions for lifelong and acquired PE. These definitions are overlapping, with three shared factors (1. Time to ejaculation assessed by IELT; 2. Perceived control; 3. Distress, bother, frustration, interpersonal difficulty related to the ejaculatory dysfunction), resulting in a multidimensional diagnosis [655].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Two more PE syndromes have been proposed [606]:\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>‘Variable PE’ is characterised by inconsistent and irregular early ejaculations, representing a normal variation in sexual performance.\u003C\u002Fli>\u003Cli>‘Subjective PE’ is characterised by subjective perception of consistent or inconsistent rapid ejaculation during intercourse, while ejaculation latency time is in the normal range or can even last longer. It should not be regarded as a symptom or manifestation of true medical pathology [656].\u003C\u002Fli>\u003C\u002Ful>\u003Ch4>6.2.5. Diagnostic evaluation\u003C\u002Fh4>\u003Cp>Diagnosis of PE is based on the patient’s medical and sexual history [657-660]. History should classify PE as lifelong or acquired and determine whether PE is situational (under specific circumstances or with a specific partner) or consistent. Special attention should be given to the duration time of ejaculation, degree of sexual stimulus, impact on sexual activity and QoL, and drug use or abuse. It is also important to distinguish PE from ED. Many patients with ED develop secondary PE caused by the anxiety associated with difficulty in attaining and maintaining an erection [613,661]. Furthermore, some patients are unaware that loss of erection after ejaculation is normal and may erroneously complain of ED, while the actual problem is PE [615].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>6.2.5.a. Intravaginal ejaculatory latency time (IELT)\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>Although it has been suggested as an objective diagnostic criterion and treatment outcome measure [662,663], the use of IELT alone is not sufficient to define PE, as there is significant overlap between men with and without PE [664,665]. Moreover, some men may experience PE in their non-coital sexual activities (e.g. during masturbation, oral sex or anal intercourse); thus, measuring IELT will not be suitable for their assessment [666]. Although PE is apparently less prevalent and less bothersome among MSM [667], many of these patients may also suffer from PE and IELT cannot be applied [667-669]. Although some studies demonstrated that MSM report longer ejaculation latency time compared to heterosexual men [667], others failed to demonstrate such a difference [670].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>In everyday clinical practice, self-estimated IELT is sufficient [671]. Self-estimated and stopwatch-measured IELT are interchangeable and correctly assign PE status with 80% sensitivity and 80% specificity [672,673].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cp>Measurement of IELT with a calibrated stopwatch is mandatory in clinical trials. For any drug treatment study of PE, Waldinger \u003Ci>et al.\u003C\u002Fi> suggested using geometric mean instead of arithmetic mean IELT because the distributed IELT data are skewed. Otherwise, any treatment-related ejaculation delay may be overestimated, if the arithmetic mean IELT is used instead of the geometric mean IELT [674].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>6.2.5.b. Premature ejaculation assessment questionnaires\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>The need to objectively assess PE has led to the development of several questionnaires based on using patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs). Only two questionnaires can discriminate between patients who have PE and those who do not:\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Cul>\u003Cli>Premature Ejaculation Diagnostic Tool (PEDT): A five-item questionnaire based on focus groups and interviews from the USA, Germany, and Spain assesses control, frequency, minimal stimulation, distress and interpersonal difficulty [675]. A total score of &gt; 11 suggests a diagnosis of PE, 9 or 10 suggests a probable diagnosis, and &lt; 8 indicates a low likelihood of PE.\u003C\u002Fli>\u003Cli>Arabic Index of Premature Ejaculation (AIPE): A seven-item questionnaire developed in Saudi Arabia assesses sexual desire, hard erections for sufficient intercourse, time to ejaculation, control, satisfaction of the patient and partner, and anxiety or depression [676]. A cut-off score of 30 (range 7-35) discriminates PE diagnosis best. Severity of PE is classified as severe (score: 7-13), moderate (score: 14-19), mild-to-moderate (score: 20-25) and mild (score: 26-30).\u003C\u002Fli>\u003C\u002Ful>\u003Cp>Other questionnaires used to characterise PE and determine treatment effects include the Premature Ejaculation Profile (PEP) [665], Index of Premature Ejaculation (IPE) [677], and Male Sexual Health Questionnaire (MSHQ) - Ejaculatory Dysfunction [678]. Currently, their role is optional in everyday clinical practice. The masturbatory PEDT has also been proposed [679], due to fact that PE patients report longer IELTs and lesser bother\u002Fdistress during masturbation than partnered sex [666]. However, further validation studies are required before the routine use of this questionnaire in this population.\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>6.2.5.c. Physical examination and investigations\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cp>Physical examination may be part of the initial assessment of men with PE. It may include a focused examination of the urological, endocrine and neurological systems to identify underlying medical conditions associated with PE or other sexual dysfunctions, such as endocrinopathy, PD, urethritis or prostatitis. Laboratory or physiological testing should be directed by specific findings from history or physical examination and is not routinely recommended [659].\u003C\u002Fp>\u003Ch5>6.2.5.d. Summary of evidence and recommendations for the diagnostic evaluation of PE\u003C\u002Fh5>\u003Cfigure class=\"table\">\u003Ctable>\u003Ctbody>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>Summary of evidence\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>\u003Cstrong>LE\u003C\u002Fstrong>\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>A comprehensive medical history and a thorough physical examination can serve as valuable tools for clinicians in identifying the underlying medical factors contributing to PE.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>3\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>PE can negatively impact self-confidence, strain partner relationships, and potentially lead to emotional distress, anxiety, shame, and depression.\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003Ctd>2a\u003C\u002Ftd>\u003C\u002Ftr>\u003Ctr>\u003Ctd>Several questionnaires can be used for the diagnosis of PE (PEDT, AIPE) and for assessing the therapeutic outcomes
